content: Compound interest follows the formula A = P(1 + r/n)^(nt), where P is principal, r is annual rate, n is compounding frequency, and t is years. The Rule of 72 provides a quick estimate: years to double ≈ 72/rate. At 8% return, money doubles every 9 years. At 12%, every 6 years. Continuous compounding uses A = Pe^(rt) and yields the theoretical maximum.

Dollar cost averaging (DCA) invests a fixed dollar amount at regular intervals regardless of price. When prices are low, you buy more shares; when high, fewer shares. DCA reduces timing risk and behavioral mistakes (panic selling, FOMO buying). Studies show lump-sum investing outperforms DCA about 66% of the time due to markets trending up, but DCA's psychological benefit of consistent investing often produces better real-world outcomes.

An emergency fund should hold 3-6 months of essential expenses in a high-yield savings account (currently 4.5-5.0% APY). Essential expenses include housing, food, insurance, transportation, and minimum debt payments. Self-employed or single-income households should target 6-12 months. The fund should be easily accessible (no CDs or brokerage accounts) and held separately from checking to avoid spending temptation.

Asset allocation by age uses the rough guideline of (100 - age) or (110 - age) in stocks, with the remainder in bonds. A 30-year-old would hold 70-80% stocks, 20-30% bonds. Modern approaches consider risk tolerance, income stability, and time horizon rather than age alone. Target-date funds (e.g., Vanguard Target 2060) automatically adjust allocation, becoming more conservative as the target date approaches.

Tax-advantaged account contribution limits for 2026: 401(k) and 403(b) at $23,500 ($31,000 if age 50+, $34,750 if age 60-63), Traditional/Roth IRA at $7,000 ($8,000 if age 50+), HSA at $4,300 individual / $8,550 family. The optimal contribution order is: 401k up to employer match → HSA max → Roth IRA max → 401k max → taxable brokerage. Each account type has different tax treatment and withdrawal rules.

Roth conversion ladder converts Traditional IRA/401k funds to Roth IRA in low-income years (sabbatical, early retirement, gap years). Converted amounts are taxed as ordinary income in the conversion year but can be withdrawn tax-free after a 5-year seasoning period. This strategy is especially powerful for early retirees who convert during the 0% or 10% tax bracket, effectively paying minimal tax on money that would later be taxed at higher rates.

Capital gains tax rates differ by holding period. Long-term gains (assets held >1 year) are taxed at 0% (income up to ~$47K single), 15% (up to ~$518K), or 20% (above ~$518K), plus a 3.8% net investment income tax for high earners. Short-term gains (held ≤1 year) are taxed as ordinary income (up to 37%). Tax-loss harvesting offsets gains with losses, with a $3,000/year deduction against ordinary income for net losses.

Index funds outperform 90% of actively managed funds over a 10-year period (SPIVA scorecard data). S&P 500 index funds (VOO, SPY, IVV) charge 0.03% expense ratio versus 0.5-1.5% for active funds. A 1% fee difference compounds dramatically: $100K invested for 30 years at 8% yields $761K with 0.03% fees versus $574K with 1% fees — a $187K difference from fees alone.

The FIRE movement (Financial Independence, Retire Early) uses the 4% rule: annual expenses x 25 = retirement number. Spending $40K/year requires a $1M portfolio. The 4% rule is based on the Trinity Study, which showed a 4% inflation-adjusted withdrawal rate survived 95% of 30-year historical periods. Critics note it assumes US-centric returns and a 30-year horizon; many FIRE adherents use 3.25-3.5% for longer retirements.

Estate planning essentials include: a will (directs asset distribution, names guardians for minors), revocable living trust (avoids probate, maintains privacy, allows incapacity management), beneficiary designations (override wills for 401k, IRA, life insurance — review annually), durable power of attorney (financial decisions if incapacitated), healthcare proxy (medical decisions), and advance directive (end-of-life wishes). Without a will, state intestacy laws determine distribution.

The debt avalanche method pays minimums on all debts and directs extra payments to the highest interest rate debt first, minimizing total interest paid. The debt snowball method pays off the smallest balance first for psychological wins. Mathematically, avalanche saves more money; behaviorally, snowball has higher completion rates. For debts above 7% interest, prioritize payoff over investing since guaranteed return exceeds expected market returns.

Credit score factors (FICO model): payment history (35%), credit utilization (30%, keep below 30%, ideal below 10%), length of credit history (15%), credit mix (10%), and new credit inquiries (10%). A score above 740 qualifies for the best interest rates. Paying statement balances in full each month, keeping old accounts open, and maintaining low utilization are the highest-impact actions for score improvement.

I Bonds (Series I Savings Bonds) combine a fixed rate (set at purchase, currently 1.2%) with an inflation rate (adjusted every 6 months based on CPI). They are tax-deferred until redemption, exempt from state/local tax, and can be tax-free if used for education. Annual purchase limit is $10,000 per person (electronic) + $5,000 (paper, via tax refund). Minimum 1-year hold, 3-month interest penalty if redeemed within 5 years.

HSA (Health Savings Account) is the only triple-tax-advantaged account: contributions are tax-deductible, growth is tax-free, and withdrawals for qualified medical expenses are tax-free. After age 65, non-medical withdrawals are taxed as ordinary income (like a Traditional IRA). The optimal strategy is to pay medical expenses out of pocket, invest HSA funds (index funds), and let them compound tax-free. HSA receipts have no expiration date — save and reimburse yourself decades later.

Umbrella insurance provides liability coverage beyond homeowner's and auto policy limits, typically in $1M increments at $150-300/year per million. It covers lawsuits from car accidents, slip-and-falls on your property, defamation, and dog bites. Recommended for anyone with net worth exceeding their auto/home liability limits. Most insurers require underlying auto liability of $250K/$500K and home liability of $300K before issuing an umbrella policy.

The 50/30/20 budget allocates after-tax income: 50% to needs (housing, food, insurance, minimum debt payments), 30% to wants (dining, entertainment, subscriptions), and 20% to savings and extra debt payments. Housing should not exceed 28% of gross income (front-end ratio) and total debt payments should not exceed 36% (back-end ratio). These thresholds are used by mortgage lenders for qualification.

Backdoor Roth IRA allows high earners above the income limit ($161K single, $240K married for 2026) to contribute to a Roth IRA by first contributing to a non-deductible Traditional IRA and then immediately converting to Roth. The pro-rata rule complicates this if you have existing pre-tax IRA balances — a portion of the conversion becomes taxable. The mega backdoor Roth uses after-tax 401k contributions converted to Roth, enabling up to $46K additional annual Roth contributions.

Life insurance needs are estimated using the DIME method: Debt (all outstanding debts), Income (annual income x years until youngest child is 18), Mortgage (remaining balance), Education (estimated college costs per child ~$100K-$300K). Term life insurance (20-30 year level term) costs $20-$50/month for $1M coverage for a healthy 30-year-old. Whole life insurance is almost never a good investment due to high fees and low returns.

Tax-loss harvesting sells investments at a loss to offset capital gains, reducing tax liability. The wash-sale rule prohibits repurchasing substantially identical securities within 30 days before or after the sale. Strategy: sell a losing total market ETF (VTI) and immediately buy a similar but not identical fund (ITOT) to maintain market exposure while realizing the tax loss. Net losses above gains can offset $3,000/year of ordinary income, with excess carried forward indefinitely.

Bond duration measures interest rate sensitivity: a bond with 5-year duration loses approximately 5% in value when interest rates rise 1%. Short-duration bonds (1-3 years) are less volatile; long-duration bonds (10-30 years) amplify rate movements. In a rising rate environment, shorter duration is safer. Bond laddering (staggering maturities, e.g., 1-5 year rungs) provides regular liquidity and averages out rate risk.

Real return equals nominal return minus inflation. If your portfolio returns 8% and inflation is 3%, your real return is approximately 5%. Over 30 years, $100K grows to $1.006M at 8% nominal but only $432K in today's purchasing power at 5% real return. All retirement planning should use real (inflation-adjusted) returns. Historical US stock real returns average about 7%, bonds about 2%.

529 plans offer tax-free growth and tax-free withdrawals for qualified education expenses (tuition, room, board, books, computers). Many states offer tax deductions for contributions ($2K-$10K per year). Unused funds can be rolled over to Roth IRA (up to $35K lifetime, subject to annual Roth contribution limits, requires 15-year minimum account age). Superfunding allows 5 years of gift contributions upfront ($90K per beneficiary in 2026).

Social Security optimization: benefits are based on the highest 35 years of indexed earnings. Full retirement age (FRA) is 67 for those born after 1960. Claiming at 62 reduces benefits by 30%; delaying to 70 increases benefits by 24% above FRA. The break-even age is approximately 80. Spousal benefits are up to 50% of the higher earner's FRA benefit. For couples, a common strategy is for the higher earner to delay to 70 while the lower earner claims earlier.

Required Minimum Distributions (RMDs) from Traditional IRAs and 401(k)s begin at age 73 (SECURE 2.0 Act, increasing to 75 in 2033). The RMD amount equals the account balance on December 31 of the prior year divided by the IRS Uniform Lifetime Table factor (e.g., 26.5 at age 73, meaning ~3.8% withdrawal). Missing RMDs triggers a 25% penalty (reduced to 10% if corrected within 2 years). Roth IRAs have no RMDs during the owner's lifetime.

Dollar-weighted return (IRR) measures the investor's actual return based on the timing and size of their cash flows, while time-weighted return (TWR) measures the investment's return independent of cash flows. A fund returning 20% then -10% has a TWR of 8%, but an investor who added money after the 20% gain and before the -10% loss would have a negative dollar-weighted return. TWR evaluates the manager; dollar-weighted evaluates the investor's decision-making.
